coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient degradation of sulindac under visible light irradiation by a novel titanium based photocatalyst
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Liyun
Li, Zhi
Cai, Yuying
Yang, Linjiao
Xie, Yuchen
Jiang, Ming
Yu, Xu
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is a kind of generally used photocatalyst with the assistance of UV light. To utilize the visible light and save the energy, herein, a titanium (Ti)-based nanocomposite, i.e. PPDs/C-hTiO2, was designed and prepared based on carbon (C)-doping and photosensitive polymer dots (PPDs) nano-hybridization. This design synergistically narrowed the band gap energy (Eg) and strengthened absorption of the visible light. As a result, PPDs/C-hTiO2 exerted remarkably high catalytic ability under visible light, surpassing that of commercial TiO2 (i.e. P25) under UV light. PPDs/C-hTiO2 succeeded in assisting the degradation of sulindac with a degradation efficiency of 96.7%±1.25% within 10 min under visible light. The degradation process was driven by the generation of hydroxyl radical, superoxide radical and holes, and the total biotoxicity of degradation products was decreased compared to the parent compound. This study creatively combined the C-doping and PPDs nano-hybridization to construct a visible light Ti-based photocatalyst, proposing a potential technique for addressing current aquatic environmental issues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2
C-doping
Photosensitive polymer dots nano-hybridization
Visible light photocatalyst
Degradation of sulindac

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Degradation of sulindac under photocatalytic condition
Evaluation of photocatalytic performance of PPDs/C-hTiO2
Application of PPDs/C-hTiO2 to assist the degradation of sulindac
pH stability and reusability of PPDs/C-hTiO2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82003423 ). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST and the Medical Subcenter of HUST Analytical & Testing Center for the material characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of synthetic conditions for the nanocomposite. The ABTS assay for nanocomposites synthesized with different (A) C-Ti: HPNPs, (B) reaction temperature and (C) reaction time. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, * p < 0.05, *** p < 0.001.

Characterization of PPDs/C-hTiO2. (A and B) TEM images; (C) SEM-EDS spectra; (D) XPS survey and high-resolution spectra of (E) Ti 2p, (F) O 1s, (G) C 1s, and (H) N 1s; (I) XRD spectra of materials as indicated.

(A) The ABTS assay in the presence of PPDs/C-hTiO2 under different wavelengths irradiation. (B) The ABTS assay in the presence of different studied materials under LED light irradiation. (C) Photocatalytic oxidation of ABTS in the presence of different scavengers. (D) EPR spectrum of DMPO-O2
− adducts of PPDs/C-hTiO2 under dark and light irradiation. (E) Transient photocurrent responses, (F) UV–vis DRS spectra, (G) Band gaps, (H) Mott-Schottky plots and (I) schematic energy-band diagrams of the studied materials. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, ***p < 0.001.

Photocatalytic performance of the studied materials towards sulindac. (A) Abatement curves and (B) pseudo-first-order kinetic curve of sulindac degradation catalyzed by different materials. (C) Removal rates of sulindac by PPDs/C-hTiO2 at different pHs. (D) Recycling tests of PPDs/C-hTiO2 in the degradation of sulindac. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

(A) Proposed transformation pathways of sulindac by PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED system. The eco-toxicity appraisement of sulindac and its transformation products to fish (B), green algae (C) and daphnid (D) by ECOSAR.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of PPDs/C-hTiO2.
Proposed mechanism for photocatalytic process towards ABTS by PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED.
The reactant, synthetic condition and name for the studied materials.

row.entry: TPT, HNO3, IPA, H2O, ascorbic acid

head.ce:title: Highly efficient degradation of sulindac under visible light irradiation by a novel titanium based photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Union Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, Union Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contribute to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is a kind of generally used photocatalyst with the assistance of UV light. To utilize the visible light and save the energy, herein, a titanium (Ti)-based nanocomposite, i.e. PPDs/C-hTiO2, was designed and prepared based on carbon (C)-doping and photosensitive polymer dots (PPDs) nano-hybridization. This design synergistically narrowed the band gap energy (Eg) and strengthened absorption of the visible light. As a result, PPDs/C-hTiO2 exerted remarkably high catalytic ability under visible light, surpassing that of commercial TiO2 (i.e. P25) under UV light. PPDs/C-hTiO2 succeeded in assisting the degradation of sulindac with a degradation efficiency of 96.7%±1.25% within 10 min under visible light. The degradation process was driven by the generation of hydroxyl radical, superoxide radical and holes, and the total biotoxicity of degradation products was decreased compared to the parent compound. This study creatively combined the C-doping and PPDs nano-hybridization to construct a visible light Ti-based photocatalyst, proposing a potential technique for addressing current aquatic environmental issues.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With modernization of the society globally, environmental pollution is becoming increasingly severe and significantly impacts on human health. Urgent action is needed to reduce and eliminate pollutants for sustainable development. For this purpose, photocatalytic degradation of pollutants is catching more attention, due to its high removal efficiency, cost-effectiveness and environmental friendliness [1–3]. The kernel of the photocatalytic technology lies in the photocatalyst, which definitely determines the catalytic efficiency and overall cost. One popular type of photocatalysts is semiconductor-based one, which activates molecular oxygen to form reactive oxygen species under the help of the solar energy [1]. Currently, several semiconductor-based photocatalysts are commercially available, including titanium dioxide (TiO2), CdS, ZnS, WO3 and g-C3N4, etc. However, these materials mainly play photocatalytic roles by absorbing ultraviolet (UV) light, which constitutes only 5% of the sun's rays [4]. To maximize the utilization of solar energy and reduce the water treatment costs, it is essential to devise sustainable photocatalysts empowered with a widened photo-responsive range and high catalytic efficiency [5–7].

TiO2 has been one of the most acceptable photocatalyst with outstanding photo-corrosion stability, low cost and non-toxicity, but it only absorbs UV light due to its broad bandgap energy (Eg) (∼3.1 eV) [8]. To harness the energy of visible light, one effective way is to narrow the Eg of TiO2 to facilitate the visible light absorption and efficient charge dissociation. Several strategies could be employed for this purpose, including development of heterojunctions, the generation of defects and the combination with photosensitizers [9]. Heterojunctions are formed by bringing two materials with distinct band structures together [10]. The existence of a heterojunction can promote the separation and prevent the recombination of photoexcited electrons (e−) and holes (h+), and thus endow TiO2 with visible light catalytic activity. However, achieving a heterojunction is challenging, because it requires a pre-understanding of the band structure and effective contact of each individual material, rather than simply mixing them together [10,11]. Another approach is to generate defects, primarily achieved by doping with elements, such as carbon (C) [12], nitrogen (N) [13], sulfur [14], fluorine [9], phosphorus [15], boron [16], or the combinations of two or more of these elements [4,17]. These elements mainly replace the oxygen (O) in TiO2, creating oxygen defect, which can not only narrow the Eg, but also facilitate charge separation and transfer. Unfortunately, element doping usually takes place under extreme conditions, such as high temperature or dangerous gases, entailing operational inconvenience and danger in practical production [4,6]. Particularly, high temperature treatment may cause the aggregation and secondary growth of TiO2, leading to a deterioration in photocatalytic activity [18]. As a solution, in recent decades, the combination of TiO2 with another photosensitizer, e.g. carbon dots (CDs) [19], polymer dots (PDs) [8] and dyestuff [20], which promote effective photo-carrier dissolution and wide-range absorption, and hence enhance photocatalytic kinetics [5], has won heightened popularity. This combination could be achieved by simply mixing TiO2 and the photosensitizer [20], decorating the photosensitizer onto TiO2 [19,21,22] or hybridizing the photosensitizer and TiO2 [8]. Although these strategies have demonstrated their effectiveness to construct Ti-based materials with visible light photocatalytic ability, the pursuit of them remains desirable [7,8,18,19]. Anyway, these three strategies could be integrated to synergistically promote the catalytic efficiency. For example, S-scheme heterojunction with the CDs modification of Cd0.5Zn0.5S/Bi2MoO6 was successful for improved anti-photo-corrosion performance and photocatalytic degradation of oxytetracycline [6]. Similarly, a Z-scheme with quantum dots in Ag3PO4/BiVO4 achieved excellent photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline [23], and an S-scheme with N-defect-modified g-C3N4/BaFe12O19 demonstrated highly efficient degradation of antibiotic [24]. However, this combination strategy was seldom used to construct Ti-based photocatalyst.

As reported, the incorporation of another heteroatom in the TiO2 precursor could introduce element doping into the final TiO2 [14]. Alternatively, some additional nanomaterials, such as quantum dots [7], PDs [8] and CDs [19], can form nanocomposites with TiO2. Herein, in this study, Ti-based nanocomposites with C-doping and photosensitive PDs (PPDs) hybridization, named as PPDs/C-hTiO2, were designed and prepared. Ascorbic acid served as the C-doping reagent for the preparation of Ti precursor, resulting in a C-doped Ti precursor (C-Ti precursor). PPDs, crosslinked by polyethyleneimine (PEI) and hydroquinone, were hybridized with TiO2 in a hydrothermal process, acting as a photosensitizer. Using this combinatory strategy, the Eg of the nanocomposite was narrowed compared to pure TiO2, enabling better absorption of visible light and achieving efficient charge dissociation, thus exerting visible-light catalytic activity. The PPDs/C-hTiO2 was further applied in the photocatalytic degradation of sulindac with a high degradation efficiency of 96.7% ± 1.25% within 10 min. The degradation was driven by the produced superoxide radicals (O2
−), hydroxyl radicals (OH) and h+, and the degradation products showed lower total biotoxicity than the parent compound. This research innovatively combined the element-doping and nano-hybridization to effectively enhance the visible light catalytic activity of TiO2, providing a potent photocatalyst for pollutant degradation.

Titanium tetraisopropanolate (TPT, purity = 97%), hydroquinone (99.0–101.0%), methanol (purity ≥ 99.5%), acetonitrile (purity ≥ 99.9%), ethanol (EtOH, purity ≥ 95.0%), nitric acid (HNO3, 65.0–68.0%), isopropanol (IPA, purity ≥ 99.7%), 1,4-benzoquinone (p-BQ, purity ≥ 98.0%), ammonium oxalate (AO, purity ≥ 99.5%), sodium chloride (NaCl, purity ≥ 99.5%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0–38.0%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, purity ≥ 96.0%), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, purity ≥ 99.0%) and barium sulfate (BaSO4, purity = 98%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ascorbic acid (purity > 99.0%), PEI (Mw = 10, 000), 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS, purity = 95%) and sulindac (purity ≥ 98.0%), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, purity ≥ 97.0%) were supplied from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The lyophilized bright luminescent bacillus powder was bought from Shang Hai Bao Cang Center (Shanghai, China). The commercial TiO2 (P25) was bought from Gongcheng Chemical (Zhejiang, China). Nafion117 was bought from Sigma-Aldrich (USA).

A JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan), a Thermo Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA), an X-ray diffractometer (XRD-6100, Shimadzu, Japan), a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, MIRA 3, TESCAN Brno, s.r.o., Russia), an Elementar UNICUBE elemental analyzer (Elementar, Germany), a TG 209 F1 Libra thermal analyzer (Netzsch, Germany) and a Mettler Toledo Delta 320 pH meter (Shanghai, China) were employed in the experiment. The O2
− generated by PPDs/C-hTiO2 was studied by an A200 Bruker electron spin (paramagnetic) resonance spectrometer (EPR, Bruker, Germany). The fluorescence intensity of luminescent bacteria was determined by an F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflection spectra (UV–vis DRS) were obtained by a UV-3600 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using BaSO4 as the reflectance standard. Purified water was prepared through a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China). Transient photocurrent response and Mott-Schottky measurements were performed on a CHI660D electrochemical workstation (ChenHua, China) with a conventional three-electrode system in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte (pH = 6.8). An Ag/AgCl, a platinum foil and an indium tin oxides (ITO) electrode deposited with tested materials were used as the reference, counter and working electrode, respectively. The ITO electrode was prepared as below. The tested materials (10 mg) were added in 1 mL 50% EtOH containing 2% Nafion117 with sonication for 30 min to form a slurry, 100 μL of which was dropped onto the surface of the ITO electrode and allowed to dry in an oven. For transient photocurrent response test, an LED lamp (λ = 420 nm) was utilized as the light source. The Mott-Schottky plots were collected at a frequency of 1000 Hz.

The concentration of sulindac was determined using an LC-20AT high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Shimadzu Co., Japan). A DIONEX ODS column (2.1 mm × 150 mm, 3 μm) was used for separation with the column temperature of 35°C. The isocratic elution was performed using 65% methanol and 35% water containing 0.1% (v/v) acetic acid at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. The injection volume was 10 μL and detection wavelength was 254 nm.

An ultra-performance liquid chromatography-quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry (UPLC-Q-TOF MS) (ACQUITY UPLC/Xevo G2XS Q-TOF MS, Waters, USA) was used to analyze the photocatalytic transformation products of sulindac. The separation was performed on a SHIMADZU Shim-pack GIST C18 column (2.1 mm × 150 mm, 3 μm) with a column temperature of 40°C. The injection volume was 1 μL. The mobile phase was 60% formic acid aqueous solution (0.1%, v/v) and 40% acetonitrile at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. The mass spectra were obtained in negative and sensitivity modes with the scanning range of 50–1200 (m/z). Other MS conditions were set as below: capillary voltage, 2.5 kV; sampling cone, 40 V; source temperature, 120°C; desolvation temperature, 400°C; cone gas flow, 50 L/h; desolvation gas flow, 800 L/h; trap gas flow, 2.0 mL/min.

The C-Ti precursor was prepared through a straightforward heating process. Briefly, 30 mg of ascorbic acid was dissolved in a mixture of water and IPA (30 mL, 1:1, v: v), followed by the addition of 1.5 mL of HNO3 and 5 mL of TPT. The mixture was heated in a water bath at 80 °C with stirring for 1 h. The C-Ti precursor was thus obtained. As a comparison, the Ti precursor was prepared using the same procedure without the addition of ascorbic acid.

Subsequently, 3 mL of C-Ti precursor was mixed with 3 mL of HPNPs aqueous dispersion (10 mg/mL) as reported in our previous work [25]. Briefly, 10 mg of hydroquinone was dissolved in 6 mL of PEI aqueous solution (0.1 g/mL) under stirring. After 6 h, the above solution was transferred into a 14, 000 Da dialysis bag and dialyzed against deionized water for a week, with water renewal every 24 h. The resulting materials were identified as HPNPs. After stirring the mixture of C-Ti precursor and HPNPs for 30 min under room temperature, it was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 6 h. The solid product was then washed by water for three times and collected by centrifugation, yielding PPDs/C-hTiO2. For comparison, controls including PPDs, C-doping TiO2 (C-hTiO2), PPDs hybridized TiO2 (PPDs/hTiO2) and TiO2 derived from Ti precursor (hTiO2) were synthesized via the hydrothermal process, as listed in Table 1 .

Generally, 4 mg of the investigated materials were added into 4 mL of ABTS aqueous solution (76.8 μg/mL) with magnetic agitation in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Afterward, the mixture was exposed to LED light, with a polarizing film for filtering out UV light. During the 1 h irradiation, 200 μL of the solution was sucked out at predetermined time intervals and immediately centrifuged. The absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 734 nm by a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of sulindac under photocatalytic condition
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical photocatalytic experiment, 30 mg of the studied catalyst were dispersed in 30 mL of sulindac aqueous solution (20 μg/mL) and magnetically agitated in the dark for 30 min to obtain adsorption–desorption equilibrium. The mixture was then subjected to LED light, with a polarizing film for filtering out UV light. At predetermined time intervals, 1 mL of the solution was collected and immediately centrifuged. The supernatant was analyzed by HPLC to obtain the residual concentration of sulindac, while the degradation products of sulindac were identified using UPLC-Q-TOF MS. As noted, this photocatalytic system, using PPDs/C-hTiO2 as the catalyst and LED as the light source, was referred as PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED.

The removal rate of sulindac was calculated according to Eq. (1), and the pseudo-first-order kinetic model was used to calculate the photocatalytic rate (k) of sulindac based on the Eq. (2).
(1)
Removal Rate (%) = (1 -
C
t
/
C
0
) × 100 %
(2)
Ln (
C t
/
C 0
) = - k t
C0 and Ct (mg/L) are the concentrations of sulindac at initial time and time t (min), respectively.

The total toxicity of transformation products was evaluated by the luminescent bacteria assay, according to GB/T 15441-1995 standard. An ampoule containing 0.5 g of freeze-dried luminous bacterial powder was opened, and 1 mL of cold NaCl solution (2%) was immediately added. After fully mixed, the solution became milky white, and was then placed in dark at 25 ± 1°C for 30 min to allow its activity to stabilize. The resuscitated bacterial suspension was used within 6 h.

To evaluate the toxicity of transformation products of sulindac, a sulindac solution (20 μg/mL) catalyzed by the PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED system for 10 min which was supposed to be completely degraded according to the above kinetics result of sulindac (Section 2.5), and blank sample (deionized water herein) were diluted 10 times with 3% NaCl solution, i.e. 0.2 mL degradation solution or deionized water were separately mixed with 1.8 mL of 3% NaCl solution, to ensure comparable osmotic pressure to that of 3% NaCl solution. Afterwards, 20 μL of the resuscitated bacterial solution was added with thorough agitation and incubated for exact 15 min at 25 ± 1°C. The luminous intensity was then determined by a fluorescence spectrometer (λex = 280 nm, λem = 480 nm). The luminous intensity inhibition rate (I%) and the percentage change in luminous intensity inhibition rate (ΔI%)% were calculated according to Eq. (3) and Eq. (4), respectively.
(3)
I % =
(
I 0
-
I X
)
/
I 0
× 100 %
(4)
( Δ I % )
% =
(
I i
% -
I P
% )
/
I i
% × 100 %

I0 and IX represent the luminous intensities of 3% NaCl solution and the sample solution, respectively. Ii% and IP% denote the luminous intensity inhibition rates of the sulindac degradation solution at the initial time and final time, respectively.

All experiments in this work were conducted in parallel in triplicate to ensure reproducibility. The quantitative data were presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). The one-way analysis of variance was calculated and * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001 suggested that the differences between the measured data were significant.

Scheme 1 illustrated the synthesis of PPDs/C-hTiO2. Thereinto, TPT served as the Ti source, and experienced hydrolysis, condensation and hybridization to form TiO2. The mixture of water and IPA provided a suitable condition for TPT hydrolysis, while HNO3 stabilized the C-Ti precursor [26].

The ascorbic acid likely acted as the carbon source, doping into the Ti structure [14], and yielding a yellow C-Ti precursor, whereas the Ti precursor without ascorbic acid appeared white (Fig. S1). Element analysis (EA) disclosed that PPDs/C-hTiO2 had the highest C content at 2.32%, compared to 2.05% for PPDs/hTiO2, 1.72% for C-hTiO2 and 0.91% for hTiO2 (Table S1). Meanwhile, the results of thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) also revealed that the PPDs/C-hTiO2 exhibited the largest weight loss of 11.43% during the temperature range of 160–620°C, while PPDs/hTiO2, C-hTiO2 and hTiO2 showed weight losses of 7.11%, 5.53% and 3.27%, respectively (Table S1). Both EA and TGA results reflected an obviously higher C content in PPDs/C-hTiO2, possibly implying that ascorbic acid was effectively doped into the Ti precursor. Compared to previously-reported methods for C doping in Ti-based material, like high-temperature calcination [13], ultrahigh pressure [27], calcination in dangerous gas (H2) [18], the current method only required mild heating (80°C) at atmospheric pressure, making it undoubtedly a simpler and safer way. As discussed later (Section 3.4), the C-doping significantly contributed to the enhanced catalytic capabilities of the final Ti-based material.

To broaden the light absorption range and enhance light absorption, the photosensitizer is typically required to possess strong absorption in the visible light region [27]. Besides absorption capability, electron transfer also plays a vital role in the photosensitization process, deciding whether the catalyst can efficiently utilize the absorbed light energy or not [28]. Furthermore, conjugated-polymer-based photosensitizers have shown significantly better performance than their small molecular analogs [29]. In order to verify the role of PPDs as photosensitizer in the visible light catalytic ability of PPDs/C-hTiO2, several experiments were conducted. Firstly, the morphologies of PPDs and HPNPs were observed. As shown in Fig. S2, the PPDs were ultra-small (<10 nm), while the HPNPs were about 10-fold larger [25]. This difference might result from the hydrothermal process, which likely facilitated the disaggregation of HPNPs into smaller PPDs. Secondly, the UV–vis absorbance spectra of HPNPs and PPDs were measured and displayed in Fig. S3. Both PPDs and HPNPs possessed strong absorption edges from 300 nm to 500 nm, but the absorption of PPDs was much stronger than that of HPNPs. Lastly, direct mixing of C-hTiO2 with HPNPs (C-hTiO2 + HPNPs) or PPDs (C-hTiO2 + PPDs) was also compared to evaluate visible light catalytic activity, as displayed in Fig. S4. In the ABTS assay, the obvious green color appeared in the presence of C-hTiO2 + PPDs demonstrating the oxidation of ABTS, but not for C-hTiO2 + HPNPs. This phenomenon illustrates the superior photosensitizer capability of PPDs over HPNPs, possibly because the hydrothermal procedure may facilitate the energy transfer from PPDs to C-hTiO2, in addition to strengthen the light absorption ability. To sum up, PPDs with strong absorption could effectively transfer absorbed light energy to C-hTiO2, acting as an efficient photosensitizer to enhance the visible light catalytic activity of C-hTiO2. It is noteworthy that, in this study, the hydrothermal treatment of HPNPs, the hydrolysis and condensation of C-Ti precursor, and hybridization of PPDs and TiO2 were accomplished in a single step (Section 2.3) with operational convenience. The obtained PPDs/C-hTiO2 exerted excellent photocatalytic activity in light of the synergistic effects of C-doping and PPDs nano-hybridization.

Under light irradiation, reactive oxidative species (ROS) would be produced in the presence of a photocatalyst, with higher ROS production indicating greater photocatalytic ability of the catalyst. Herein, the ABTS assay was selected to estimate the photocatalytic activity of the studied materials, guiding the optimization of synthetic conditions. To achieve the best photocatalytic performance, the volume proportion between the C-Ti precursor and HPNPs (C-Ti: HPNPs), along with reaction temperature and reaction time, were systematically optimized. The C-Ti: HPNPs ratio directly influenced the hybridization of the nanocomposites, making it the first focus of investigation. As the C-Ti: HPNPs ratio decreased (elevated proportion of HPNPs), the colors of the materials changed from white to brown intuitively (Fig. S5), suggesting successful hybridization of PPDs and C-hTiO2. However, the catalytic capability initially strengthened and then weakened (Fig. 1 A). This observation can be explained as follows. When the proportion of HPNPs was low, the amount of it hybridized with C-Ti precursor was minimal, leading to weak assistance to the catalytic ability of TiO2. With the proportion of HPNPs elevating, more PPDs were hybridized with C-hTiO2, and thus the photocatalytic activity was enhanced. However, when C-Ti: HPNPs exceeded a certain threshold, such as 1:5, the photocatalytic activity became weaker again. The hybridization of PPDs and C-hTiO2 might regulate the oxygen vacancies (OVs) on TiO2, which is essential for producing O2
− and thus influences the efficiency of photo-generated charge separation during photocatalytic procedure [30]. Based on the results in Fig. 1A, when the C-Ti: HPNPs was 3:3, PPDs/C-hTiO2 performed the best catalytic ability, possibly ascribed to the richest OVs in the final Ti-based material [30].

Another two important factors affecting the formation of nanocomposites, including reaction temperature and time, were also studied. As reaction temperature elevated or reaction time extended, the catalytic activity of the material increased first and then decreased (Fig. 1B and C). Consequently, the reaction temperature of 180°C and reaction time of 6 h were selected as the optimal conditions. Anyway, although the hydrothermal process was complex and the formation of PPDs/C-hTiO2 remained unclear, all the above factors collectively influenced the catalytic activity of the resulting materials.

The PPDs/C-hTiO2 obtained under optimal condition was fully characterized. As displayed in the TEM images (Fig. 2 A and B), PPDs/C-hTiO2 appeared as irregular spheres similar to C-hTiO2 (Fig. S6A and B). To ascertain the elemental compositions and chemical state of the materials, SEM-EDS and XPS measurements were performed. As disclosed by SEM-EDS images (Fig. 2C), C, N, O and Ti were evenly distributed in PPDs/C-hTiO2. Copious C (C 1s, 284.7 eV, 19.16%), Ti (Ti 2p, 458.3 eV, 25.17%) and O (O 1s, 529.6 eV, 53.92%) species were identified in PPDs/C-hTiO2 (Fig. 2D), along with less presence of N (N 1s, 400.0 eV, 1.75%), likely originating from PPDs. This could be also manifested by the EA result (Table S1), as the N content was increased to 0.25% (PPDs/hTiO2) and 0.27% (PPDs/C-hTiO2) only after hybridization with PPDs. Fig. 2E shows that the binding energies of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 peaks for PPDs/C-hTiO2 appeared at 458.3 eV and 464.0 eV, separately, which normally existed at ∼459.3 eV and ∼464.5 eV [4,9,12], respectively. This slight blue shift may be related to the C and N doping concentrations and concomitantly-introduced oxygen defects [18]. The O 1s spectrum of PPDs/C-hTiO2 (Fig. 2F) displayed two characteristic peaks at 529.6 eV and 530.7 eV, corresponding to O in TiO2 lattice and OH groups on the TiO2 surface, respectively. In the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2G), CC (284.3 eV), CO (285.8 eV) and TiOC (288.0 eV) were obvious, disclosing the presence of C atoms in the lattice of TiO2. Furthermore, a broad peak extending from ∼396.0 to ∼403.0 eV in the N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2H) was observed for PPDs/C-hTiO2, which was assigned to the interstitial doping of N, i.e. TiNO [4,10,21,22]. The XRD spectrum (Fig. 2I) confirmed that PPDs/C-hTiO2, PPDs/hTiO2, C-hTiO2 and hTiO2 all exhibited a mixed anatase/rutile phase TiO2, with ratios of anatase to rutile about 4:1, 2:4, 3:4 and 1:4 (calculated by Jade 6), respectively. When the ratio of anatase to rutile phase was close to 4:1, the TiO2 based material was supposed to have the highest catalytic activity [31], which might be one of the underlying reasons contributing to the strongest catalytic activity of PPDs/C-hTiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of photocatalytic performance of PPDs/C-hTiO2
ce:section.ce:para: PPDs/C-hTiO2 was basically a Ti based material in nature, and expected to act as a photocatalyst. The photocatalytic activity of PPDs/C-hTiO2 was assessed under different irradiation wavelengths using the ABTS assay (Section 2.4). As the irradiation wavelengths elongated from 365 to 470 nm, a corresponding rise in absorbance was resulted (Fig. 3 A), indicating a higher production of ox-ABTS. This finding obviously demonstrated the strong photocatalytic activity of PPDs/C-hTiO2 under visible light, dependent on the wavelength. This photocatalytic activity may be owing to the C-doping and nano-hybridization that dramatically narrowed the Eg of TiO2 [12,17,22], thus leading to the enhanced visible light catalytic activity (Section 3.4). Additionally, the catalytic efficiency of PPDs/C-hTiO2 under 470 nm (visible light) was about 2-fold better than that of P25 under 365 nm (UV light) (data not shown), further illustrating the excellent catalytic ability of PPDs/C-hTiO2 under visible light.

To better simulate daily light situations, LED light was instead utilized to further investigate the photocatalytic activity of PPDs/C-hTiO2. The catalytic kinetics of ABTS with individual studied materials as catalyst was depicted in Fig. 3B. Obviously, PPDs, C-hTiO2 and P25 nearly could not catalyze the oxidation of ABTS under LED light irradiation, while PPDs/C-hTiO2 and PPDs/hTiO2 could effectively oxidize ABTS to ox-ABTS. This phenomenon revealed that PPDs hybridization was crucial to endow the material photocatalytic activity under the visible light, possibly due to the photosensitizer role of PPDs as discussed above (Section 3.1). Additionally, the photocatalytic activity of PPDs/C-hTiO2 was remarkably stronger than that of PPDs/hTiO2, attributing to C-doping by ascorbic acid. Although C-doping alone could not directly endow TiO2 with visible light catalytic activity, as C-hTiO2 possessed almost no catalytic ability under these conditions (Fig. 3B), it may facilitate the separation of e− and h+, electron transfer or inhibit the reintegration of photo-carriers, thus enhancing the catalytic ability. Synergistically, the combination of C-doping and PPDs nano-hybridization greatly enhanced the photocatalytic ability of PPDs/C-hTiO2.

The AO, p-BQ and IPA assays were conducted to investigate the occurrence of h+, O2
− and OH respectively, in the oxidation of ABTS catalyzed by PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED (Fig. 3C). Obviously, the oxidation of ABTS was inhibited in the presence of AO, p-BQ and IPA by 81.5%, 84.9% and 9.1% in this order. This suggested that O2
− and h+ contributed prominently to the catalytic ability of PPDs/C-hTiO2. The involvement and creation of O2
− radicals in the photocatalytic process were further testified by the EPR measurements. DMPO could capture O2
− in the solution to form DMPO-O2
−, generating an EPR signal. Notably, PPDs/C-hTiO2 delivered the prominent signals of DMPO-O2
− (Fig. 3D), indicating the presence of O2
− radicals. These results provided evidences that the irradiation induced the separation of e− and h+, and thus generated h+, O2
− and OH for the oxidation of ABTS (Eqs. 5–8).
(5)
PPDs / C-hTi
O 2
+ hv →
e
-
+
h
+
(6)
O 2
+
e
-
→
O
2
· -
(7)

+
+
H 2
O → · OH
(8)
(
O
2
· -
,
· OH ,
h
+
) + ABTS → o x -ABTS

In order to understand the underlying reasons for the improved photocatalytic ability of PPDs/C-hTiO2, the transport and dissolution dynamics of photo-carriers were examined by transient photocurrent response analysis. In comparison to PPDs/hTiO2, C-hTiO2 and hTiO2, PPDs/C-hTiO2 provided a noticeably stronger photocurrent intensity (Fig. 3E), suggesting that the photo-carriers were more successfully detached and migrated in the case of PPDs/C-hTiO2, and thus the strongest photocatalytic activity (Fig. 3B). The finding indicated that C-doping with ascorbic acid and PPDs hybridization could synergistically contribute to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of TiO2.

Moreover, the energy band structures of materials were analyzed by UV–vis DRS and Mott-Schottky measurement. As depicted in Fig. 3F, the visible-light absorption decreased in the order of PPDs/C-hTiO2, PPDs/hTiO2, C-hTiO2 and hTiO2, indicating that both C-doping and PPDs nano-hybridization contributed to the strengthened absorbance. As illustrated in Fig. 3G, calculations based on the Kubelka-Munk equation revealed that the Eg values for PPDs/C-hTiO2 and PPDs/hTiO2 were 1.70 eV and 1.96 eV, respectively, which were visibly lower than those of C-hTiO2 (2.73 eV) and hTiO2 (2.83 eV).

Furthermore, the semiconductor type and the corresponding flat-band potential (Efb) were determined by the Mott-Schottky measurement. As illustrated in Fig. 3H, the Mott-Schottky plots of PPDs/C-hTiO2, PPDs/hTiO2, C-hTiO2 and hTiO2 exhibited positive slopes, suggesting the classical n-type semiconductor traits of these materials [32]. Meanwhile, the Efb values in the reference to Ag/AgCl (Efb(Ag/AgCl)) were determined as −1.01, −1.17, −1.34 and −1.44 eV (pH = 7), respectively. Consequently, the Efb values with respect to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) (Efb(NHE)) were −0.813, −0.973, −1.143 and −1.243 eV, respectively (Efb(NHE) = Efb(Ag/AgCl) + 0.197 eV). Generally, the Efb(NHE) of n-type semiconductor is approximate to the conduction band (CB) potential (ECB). Thus, the valence band (VB) potential (EVB) for PPDs/C-hTiO2, PPDs/hTiO2, C-hTiO2 and hTiO2 were estimated to be 0.887, 0.987, 1.587 and 1.587 eV, respectively (Eg = EVB − ECB) [32]. Based on the above measurements and calculations, the energy bands of the four materials were depicted in Fig. 3I. PPDs/C-hTiO2 possessed the strongest visible-light absorption ability and the narrowest Eg, both of which favored light absorption, promoted photoinduced carrier generation, increased electron excitation, improved the separation of h+ and e−, and thus led to excellent photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PPDs/C-hTiO2 to assist the degradation of sulindac
ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical and personal care products pollution poses a significant challenge to environmental sustainability [6]. Non-steroid anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) have been extensively utilized in aquaculture and human therapies. Due to frequent human use and inadequate disposal practices, residual NSAIDs have become prevalent in the environment, with concentrations ranging from ng/L to μg/L [1]. The increasing occurrence of NSAIDs in environment poses risks to human health, as these biologically active compounds may harm non-target organisms. Thus, it is crucial to effectively remove residual NSAIDs [1]. Among them, sulindac is prescribed as an effective anti-inflammatory and clinically chemopreventive agent [33], which was claimed to exert obvious toxicity on aquatic luminescent bacteria and exhibited high environmental persistence [34,35]. Herein, sulindac served as a model pharmaceutical pollutant for degradation assisted by PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED.

The applicability of PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED to catalyze the degradation of sulindac was herein investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, after 10 min irradiation by the LED light, the removal rates of sulindac under aqueous solution (pH 4.5) and ambient conditions of 25°C by PPDs/C-hTiO2, PPDs/hTiO2, C-hTiO2, PPDs and P25 reached 96.7% ± 1.2%, 86.4% ± 0.15%, 36.7% ± 0.71%, 2.62% ± 0.21% and 87.2% ± 0.11%, respectively. To be noted, although P25 seemed to exert favorable removal capacity towards sulindac within 10 min, this effect was largely ascribed to the adsorption of sulindac onto P25, rather than actual degradation. As manifested in Fig. 4A, the adsorption quantities of sulindac on the other four studied materials in the dark were minimal in 30 min (<10%), while P25 could adsorb as much as up to 65.5% ± 0.68%. The pseudo-first-order kinetic model was utilized to fit the photocatalytic degradation process (Fig. 4B), with the maximal photocatalytic degradation rate of 0.319 min−1 for PPDs/C-hTiO2. These results indicated that sulindac degradation was rapid and complete with the assistance of PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH stability and reusability of PPDs/C-hTiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Under practical circumstance, the surrounding pH is essential for the applicability of the catalyst, which can affect the catalytic process to a great extent. Hence, the photocatalytic performance of PPDs/C-hTiO2 towards sulindac was evaluated at different pHs (Fig. 4C). The removal rates of sulindac were 96.7% ± 1.2%, 96.10% ± 0.38%, 94.0% ± 0.71% and 48.1% ± 1.2% at pHs of 4.5, 7.0, 9.0 and 11.0, respectively. These results indicated that PPDs/C-hTiO2 could exert favorable catalytic ability under acid, neutral and slightly alkalescent conditions, but perform poorly under basic condition. Additionally, the removal efficiency of sulindac by PPDs/C-hTiO2 in 10 min was higher than 89% by 420 min UV irradiation [34], indicating the efficient photocatalytic activity of PPDs/C-hTiO2. Anyway, considering that the pH of most natural waters is typically neutral or weakly acidic [36], PPDs/C-hTiO2 shows great promise for practice applications.

Anti-photo-corrosion is another important property that usually determines the reusability of the catalyst for cost effectiveness and sustainability. Herein, the removal rates of sulindac using reused PPDs/C-hTiO2 over five repetitive catalytic cycles were determined (Fig. 4D), showing almost constant rates. This indicates the stability and reusability of PPDs/C-hTiO2, likely due to the nano-hybridization of PPDs and hTiO2, which permitted the efficient depletion of photo-generated holes in TiO2, thus successfully suppressing photo-corrosion and favoring the stability of PPDs/C-hTiO2 [5].

To elucidate the transformation pathway of sulindac, UPLC-Q-TOF MS analysis was performed to identify the degradation products of sulindac exposed to 10 min visible light irradiation, resulting in nearly complete degradation of sulindac according to its kinetics results. Table S2 and Fig. S7 list the UPLC-Q-TOF MS results of sulindac and its 15 potential transformation products.

Based on the above results, four degradation routes of sulindac were proposed in Fig. 5 A. In pathway I, owing to its nucleophilic property, O2
− is prone to attack the position with high positive charge [37], i.e. the position of carbon atom connected to fluorine atom. As a result, the insert of O2
− into the benzene ring structure generated product P1 [1]. The five-membered ring of P1 was cleaved, leading to the formation of P2. Meanwhile, P1 could be also hydrolyzed to generate P3. P4 and P5 were separately generated from P1 and P2 through OH electrophilic reaction [38,39], with substitution typically occurring at benzene sites with higher electron cloud density [38]. Afterwards, P4 underwent hydrolysis and decarboxylation subsequently under the attack of h+ to yield P6. Of course, P4 might undergo ring opening to form P5. On the other hand, the methyl group on the five-membered ring of sulindac might be oxidized to a carboxyl group by O2
−, generating P7.

In pathway II, considering the possibility that the benzenes of sulindac could be directly attacked by OH and the existence of steric hindrance effect, P8 and P9 were thus generated. These two compounds further underwent decarboxylation by the attack of h+, resulting in P10 and P11, respectively. Additionally, with the attack of h+, sulindac might lose –COOH directly to produce P12, i.e. pathway III. Then, P10 and P13 were formed by OH or O2
− attack, respectively. As for pathway IV, sulindac might also lose a methylene to generate P14, which could also undergo further decarboxylation to yield P15 [38].

The ECOSAR software was employed to evaluate the acute toxicity of each transformation product of sulindac against three aquatic organisms, i.e. fish, daphnid and green algae. As shown in Fig. 5B, the lethal concentration 50% (LC50) of sulindac to fish was 26.4 mg/L, while LC50 values of most degradation products ranged from 1 to 47 times higher than that of sulindac. Notably, P14 (69.9 mg/L) and P8 (27.8 mg/L) exerted less harm and P7 (1240.3 mg/L), P2 (686.0 mg/L), P1 (682.4 mg/L), P3 (651.8 mg/L), P13 (129.4 mg/L), P5 (118.2 mg/L) and P4 (117.6 mg/L) exerted no harm to fish. Conversely, a few products, including P6, P15, P10, P12, P9 and P11, exhibited greater toxicity than sulindac (Fig. 5B). Similarly, for daphnid and green algae, a large proportion of photocatalytic transformation products exhibited weaker toxicity than that of sulindac (Fig. 5C and D).

Furthermore, the luminous bacteria assay was adopted to estimate the overall toxicity of the transformation products. If the fluorescence intensity of luminous bacteria co-incubated with the parent drug is weaker than that of luminous bacteria co-incubated with transformation products, it indicates that the total toxicity of the transformation products is weaker than that of the parent drug. As displayed in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensities of photocatalytic products of sulindac were stronger than that of sulindac, demonstrating the lower toxicity of transformation products. In order to quantify the toxicities, I% was calculated (red column in Fig. S8), whereas a smaller I% indicates lower toxicity to the luminescent bacteria. After treated with the PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED system for 10 min, the I% of the degradation solution was 19.1% ((ΔI%)%) lower than that of the parent drug, illustrating that the photocatalytic degradation products were less toxic. The result of the luminous bacteria assay demonstrates that the photocatalytic degradation of sulindac by the PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED system could reduce the toxicity of sulindac to some extent, which aligns closely with the findings from ECOSAR evaluation.

In this study, a novel titanium (Ti)-based nanocomposite incorporating carbon (C)-doping and photosensitive polymer dots (PPDs), i.e. PPDs/C-hTiO2, was designed and prepared through a relatively straightforward method. PPDs/C-hTiO2 demonstrated strong visible-light absorption and a reduced bandgap energy, both of which promoted the light absorption and photoinduced carrier generation, increased electron excitation and improved separation of holes and electron, resulting in excellent photocatalytic activity. This photocatalytic ability of PPDs/C-hTiO2 under visible light was higher than that of commercial TiO2 under UV light. In light of this ability, PPDs/C-hTiO2 was successfully applied to assist the degradation of sulindac, achieving a high removal efficiency of 96.7% ± 1.25% within 10 min and a photocatalytic rate of 0.319 min−1, surpassing previously reported values. Fifteen transformation products were identified with lowered total toxicity. The produced radicals, containing superoxide radical, hydroxyl radical and h+ with the assistance of PPDs/C-hTiO2/LED, were demonstrated to participate in the transformation of sulindac. Moreover, the photo-corrosion of PPDs/C-hTiO2 was adorable, which could be recycled without obviously reduced photocatalytic effectiveness. In our ongoing study, we will attempt to use this photocatalyst to degrade other residual common drugs in the environment, such as antibiotics, pesticides, and hormones, etc. In summary, this study provided an important new insight into the design of stable and effective visible light Ti-based photocatalysts for the removal of organic pollutants from the environment by the combination of element-doping and nano-hybridization.

Liyun Ma: Writing – original draft, Resources, Formal analysis. Zhi Li: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Yuying Cai: Data curation. Linjiao Yang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuchen Xie: Data curation. Ming Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Xu Yu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Li Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82003423). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST and the Medical Subcenter of HUST Analytical & Testing Center for the material characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.11.089.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
